candidates.title: Multi-Resolution Diagnostic Framework (BCS + GPTA + LP Mixing)
candidates.summary: Three-level diagnostic: gradient-free BCS for fast screening, gradient-based GPTA for precise quantification, and influence-linearized minimax LP for optimal mixing. Falsification-first experimental design with C-LOTO ground truth, pre-registered thresholds, architecture-vs-data ablation, and OOD generalization test.
candidates.pilot_focus: Quick LOO on 5 LIBERO tasks to detect negative transfer signal; BCS computation and visualization; GPTA vs. 5 LOO correlation check
key_strengths: Multi-resolution design covers fast/cheap to precise/actionable
Falsification-first methodology makes every outcome publishable
Architecture ablation addresses contrarian's strongest objection
Formal theory (spectral interaction theorem, minimax optimality) elevates beyond application paper
key_risks: LIBERO-10 may lack strong negative transfer (35% probability)
Gradient-based proxies may be fragile for policy networks (30%)
Per-task LoRA may dominate data mixing (45%)

candidates.title: Positive Transfer Cartography + Foundation Demonstrations
candidates.summary: Map the positive transfer landscape in multi-task VLA. Identify 'universal-positive' demonstrations that improve all tasks simultaneously. Curate a minimal demonstration set that provides strong initialization for any downstream task. Directly connects to AgiBot World diversity findings.
hypotheses: Positive transfer is stronger and more common than negative transfer
A curated 30% subset of demonstrations matches full-data training
Single-embodiment advantage explained by positive transfer structure
candidates.pilot_focus: After computing influence matrix, check ratio of positive vs. negative entries. If positive dominates, pivot here.
key_strengths: Connects to hot topic (data curation for foundation models)
key_risks: Less novelty if CUPID/SCIZOR already covers instance-level curation
May require larger-scale experiments for convincing results

candidates.title: Architecture vs. Data: When Does Shared Multi-Task Learning Pay Off?
candidates.summary: Systematic empirical study comparing shared-model training with influence-guided mixing vs. per-task LoRA isolation across axes of task similarity, model capacity, and number of tasks. Provides a practitioner decision tree.
hypotheses: H5: Per-task LoRA beats shared model for known tasks
Shared model wins only for open-vocabulary generalization
Crossover point exists at a specific task-similarity threshold
candidates.pilot_focus: Quick comparison of shared model vs. per-task LoRA on 2-3 tasks from Phase 3 architecture ablation.
key_strengths: Addresses the most devastating contrarian challenge
Highly practical -- gives practitioners clear guidance
key_risks: May be seen as 'just an ablation study' without enough novelty
Regime boundaries may be fuzzy rather than sharp

meta.decision_rationale: Front-runner (cand_a) synthesizes the strongest elements from all 6 perspectives: innovator's multi-resolution insight, pragmatist's BCS/GPTA methods, theorist's formal guarantees, contrarian's falsification-first design, interdisciplinary's ecological stability analysis, and empiricist's rigorous experimental protocol. Backups cover the two most likely failure modes: negative transfer is weak (cand_b) or architecture dominates data mixing (cand_c). No candidates dropped at this stage -- all three are viable and produce publishable results.
